response:
Sarah owns a small retail shop that supports local factory workers by selling their handmade products. She decides to run a promotional week where she offers a 15% discount on all items produced by the factory workers. If a customer purchases a handcrafted table originally priced at $200 and a set of factory-made chairs originally priced at $150, how much does the customer pay for these items during the promotional week? Remember to apply the discount to each item separately. ***

During the promotional week, the customer will pay 15% less for each item. The handcrafted table originally priced at $200 will cost $200 - (15% of $200) = $200 - $30 = $170. The factory-made chairs originally priced at $150 will cost $150 - (15% of $150) = $150 - $22.50 = $127.50. The total amount the customer pays for both items is $170 + $127.50 = $297.50. Reach for the discount, the customer saves $30 on the table and $22.50 on the chairs, totaling $52.50 saved. The customer pays $297.50 for the items during the promotional week.

*The customer saves $52.50 on the two items during the promotional week.* The promotional week offers a 15% discount on all items, which means the customer pays 85% of the original prices. The handcrafted table originally priced at $200 will cost $170, and the factory-made chairs originally priced at $150 will cost $127.50. The total amount the customer pays for both items is $297.50. The customer saves $30 on the table and $22.50 on the chairs, totaling $52.50 saved. The customer pays $297.50 for the items during the promotional week.
